+# Shipping substrates v2 — bench harness + focused security review
+
+Status: **draft, /loop pass 1**
+Branch: `research/management-ui`
+Date: 2026-04-26
+Scope: `gcs-backend/` (ADR-155), `ipfs-backend/` (ADR-005), `mcp-server/` (ADR-004 v0.8)
+
+This document is the bench-harness + focused-security counterpart to the
+new-substrate (rvdna, ruqu) reviews being added in parallel. It covers the
+three already-shipping substrates so the /loop directive
+("benchmarked, security reviewed, optimized") applies to them too.
+
+Method:
+- Each crate gets ONE focused criterion bench targeting the load-bearing
+  in-process op. Network and live-service paths are excluded — the goal is
+  to lock down a per-substrate baseline that subsequent /loop passes can
+  detect regression against, not to measure GCS or kubo.
+- Security review focuses on five buckets: input validation at the
+  boundary, panic-on-poison / lock starvation, witness-equality
+  assumptions, JWT scope leakage (mcp-server only), and resource bounds.
+- No substrate `src/` files were modified by this pass. Findings that
+  would require code changes are recorded as Recommendations, not
+  applied.
+
+Hardware: `ruvultra` (the host the operator commits from). Numbers below
+are wall-clock from criterion v0.5 with `--warm-up-time 1
+--measurement-time 3 --sample-size 20-30`. They are baselines for
+regression tracking, not absolute claims.
+
+---
+
+## 1. `gcs-backend/` — ADR-155 §M2 (Parquet on GCS)
+
+### 1.1 Bench results
+
+Bench file: `gcs-backend/benches/pull_vectors.rs`
+Run: `cargo bench --manifest-path gcs-backend/Cargo.toml`
+
+| Bench                                         | n × dim       | median        | throughput        |
+| --------------------------------------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ----------------- |
+| `gcs_pull_vectors_in_memory`                  | 100  × 16     | 18.74 µs      | 325.7 MiB/s       |
+| `gcs_pull_vectors_in_memory`                  | 1000 × 64     | 184.78 µs     | 1.29 GiB/s        |
+| `gcs_pull_vectors_in_memory`                  | 1000 × 384    | 742.01 µs     | 1.93 GiB/s        |
+| `gcs_current_bundle_cheap_path` (dim known)   | —             | 963 ns        | n/a               |
+
+Notes:
+- Throughput is computed against the *vector-payload* footprint
+  (`n * dim * 4 B`) not the on-wire Parquet size; the parquet-decode
+  + arrow→`PulledBatch` conversion clearly scales sub-linearly with row
+  count (the per-row cost flattens as the batch amortises footer/decoder
+  setup).
+- The cheap-path `current_bundle()` at <1 µs confirms the ADR-004
+  §Resources contract holds: the override does NOT fall through to
+  `pull_vectors`. A regression here (e.g. a future refactor that
+  accidentally drops the operator-supplied `dim` short-circuit) would
+  show up as a 4-6 order-of-magnitude jump.
+- The GCS network round-trip is **not measured** — `object_store::memory::InMemory`
+  is the substrate. A subsequent /loop pass with `RULAKE_GCS_LIVE_TEST=1`
+  can layer on real-bucket numbers.
+
+### 1.2 Security review
+
+**Input validation at boundary (`pull_vectors`)**
+- `dim > MAX_PULLED_DIM` is rejected (backend.rs:203). Good — bounds
+  the per-row Vec allocation.
+- `DimensionMismatch` is raised when row widths disagree
+  (backend.rs:398). Good — defends against a corrupted Parquet file
+  that mixes row widths within a list-typed column.
+- The decoder downcasts to `Float32Array` / `ListArray` /
+  `FixedSizeList` and returns `RuLakeError::Backend` (not panic) on
+  type mismatch. Good — a Parquet file with a deliberately mistyped
+  `vector` column (e.g. `LIST<DOUBLE>`) lands in the error path.
+- **No `n_rows` cap**. A 100 GB Parquet object would be read fully into
+  RAM. ADR-155 §M2 puts the cache layer above us — but the backend
+  itself has no per-pull row ceiling. See Recommendation R-GCS-1.
+
+**Panic-on-poison / lock starvation**
+- `inner.collections` and `inner.schema_cache` use `RwLock` with
+  `.unwrap()` (backend.rs:111, 122, 133, 139). A poisoned lock will
+  panic. Reasoning is sound (any panic *while holding* these locks
+  reflects a corrupted invariant; recovering would silently expose
+  partial state) — but document it.
+- The blocking `runtime.block_on(...)` calls hold neither lock, so
+  there's no risk of an async-blocking-sync inversion across them.
+
+**Witness-equality assumptions**
+- The bundle here lives in core-lib (`RuLakeBundle::new` →
+  SHAKE-256(32)). The GCS backend just feeds inputs (`data_ref`,
+  `dim`, `seed`, `rerank_factor`, `Generation::Num(generation)`).
+- `generation_of(meta)` falls back to `last_modified.timestamp() as
+  u64` when `meta.version` is absent or unparsable (backend.rs:332-341).
+  In test (`InMemory`) this gives second-resolution generations; in
+  prod GCS always supplies a numeric `version`. **The fallback is
+  audit-silent** — a misconfigured bucket that strips the version
+  header would silently degrade cache coherence to second-granularity.
+  See Recommendation R-GCS-2.
+
+**Findings**
+
+| Sev | Title                                                                | Status                |
+| --- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- |
+| Lo  | `pull_vectors` has no row-count cap                                  | Open (R-GCS-1)        |
+| Lo  | Generation fallback to `last_modified` is silent on misconfig        | Open (R-GCS-2)        |
+| Inf | RwLock unwrap is intentional and documented — accept                  | Resolved by review    |
+
+**Recommendations**
+
+- **R-GCS-1**: Add a `max_rows` ceiling (operator-configurable, default
+  e.g. 50M) to `pull_vectors`, surfaced as
+  `RuLakeError::InvalidParameter` once the in-progress decode crosses
+  it. Cuts the worst-case-OOM blast radius from "size of the largest
+  bucket object" to a known constant.
+- **R-GCS-2**: Emit `tracing::warn!` once per (bucket, object) when
+  `meta.version` is absent and we fall through to `last_modified`.
+  Operators auditing for cache-coherence regressions today have no
+  signal that the fallback fired.
+
+---
+
+## 2. `ipfs-backend/` — ADR-005 (witness-anchored bundle distribution)
+
+### 2.1 Bench results
+
+Bench file: `ipfs-backend/benches/verify_bundle.rs`
+Run: `cargo bench --manifest-path ipfs-backend/Cargo.toml`
+
+| Bench                                              | dim    | median        | throughput  |
+| -------------------------------------------------- | ------ | ------------- | ----------- |
+| `ipfs_verify_witness`                              | 8      | 693.94 ns     | n/a         |
+| `ipfs_verify_witness`                              | 128    | 726.87 ns     | n/a         |
+| `ipfs_verify_witness`                              | 1024   | 691.81 ns     | n/a         |
+| `ipfs_bundle_json_roundtrip`                       | 8      | 1.094 µs      | 297.2 MiB/s |
+| `ipfs_bundle_json_roundtrip`                       | 128    | 1.218 µs      | 268.5 MiB/s |
+| `ipfs_bundle_json_roundtrip`                       | 1024   | 1.082 µs      | 303.1 MiB/s |
+| `ipfs_current_bundle_cheap_path` (operator CID+dim)| —      | 841.7 ns      | n/a         |
+
+Notes:
+- Witness recomputation is `dim`-invariant (~700 ns) as expected — the
+  SHAKE-256 input is a fixed-shape canonical encoding of bundle fields,
+  not the vector body.
+- The full JSON round-trip (serialize → bytes → from_json with size
+  caps → verify) sustains ~270-300 MiB/s. With the `from_json` 64 KiB
+  cap that's a hard ceiling of ~250k bundle parses/sec per core.
+- Cheap-path `current_bundle()` at ~840 ns confirms ADR-005 §4: when
+  the operator pre-sets CID + dim, no kubo/gateway HTTP call is made.
+  Live IPFS round trips are excluded by design (a mock-kubo bench would
+  measure hyper, not the substrate).
+
+### 2.2 Security review
+
+**Input validation at boundary**
+- `validate_cid_shape` (backend.rs:511) rejects empty / whitespace /
+  control-byte CIDs at register time. Good first line.
+- The actual CID multibase + multihash validation is delegated to
+  kubo / the gateway, by design ("no `cid` crate; canonical validator
+  is on the wire"). This is **defensible** because every kubo/gateway
+  response surfaces an error for malformed CIDs — an attacker who
+  smuggles a junk CID through `register` would still get a
+  `RuLakeError::Backend` on the first `cat`.
+- `from_json` enforces 64 KiB hard cap and 4 KiB per-field cap on the
+  way back from `cat` (`bundle.rs:218`, `bundle.rs:243+`). Good.
+  This caps the tail-risk of a maliciously-pinned bundle that would
+  otherwise force unbounded allocation in serde.
+- The fetched bundle's `data_ref` is compared to `format!("ipfs://{cid}")`
+  but a mismatch only emits a `tracing::warn!` (backend.rs:283-289) —
+  it does NOT refuse the bundle. The witness still has to verify per
+  `RuLakeBundle::from_json`'s closing path, so the bundle isn't
+  *silently* trusted, but operators reading the audit log might miss
+  the warning. See Recommendation R-IPFS-1.
+
+**Panic-on-poison / lock starvation**
+- `inner.collections` `RwLock` is `.unwrap()`d throughout — same
+  Rationale as gcs-backend. Acceptable.
+- `inner.runtime.block_on(...)` runs `cat` / `add` / `pin/add`
+  futures. The reqwest client has no operator-configurable timeout
+  (it gets the default). A misbehaving kubo (slow-response or
+  half-open TCP) could pin a calling thread. See R-IPFS-2.
+
+**Witness-equality assumptions**
+- The witness check is `expected == self.rvf_witness` (`bundle.rs:199`),
+  i.e. byte-equality on the SHAKE-256(32) hex string. That's correct
+  given the witness is canonical hex — there's no leading-zero
+  ambiguity, no case-insensitivity gap (the encoder is fixed-case).
+- `Generation::Opaque(cid)` is the IPFS-side generation — string
+  compare is fine because CIDs are content-addressed.
+- The `data_ref ≠ ipfs://{cid}` warn-only path is the ONE place where
+  the witness still verifies but the *meaning* of the bundle (which
+  CID it claims to be) drifts from where it was actually fetched.
+  Witness equality alone doesn't close that gap.
+
+**Findings**
+
+| Sev | Title                                                                  | Status              |
+| --- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- |
+| Med | `data_ref` mismatch is warn-only, not refuse                           | Open (R-IPFS-1)     |
+| Lo  | reqwest client has no operator-tunable timeout for cat / add / pin     | Open (R-IPFS-2)     |
+| Inf | CID validation deferred to kubo — defensible, document explicitly      | Resolved by review  |
+
+**Recommendations**
+
+- **R-IPFS-1**: Promote the `data_ref ≠ ipfs://{cid}` mismatch from
+  `tracing::warn!` to a hard `RuLakeError::Backend`. The witness check
+  alone doesn't catch a CID-substitution attack where the attacker
+  re-pinned a *legitimately-witnessed* bundle under a different CID.
+  This is a small change (1-2 lines, `backend.rs:283-289`) and closes
+  the only remaining trust gap in the read path.
+- **R-IPFS-2**: Surface a `connect_timeout` + `request_timeout` on the
+  reqwest client builder (`backend.rs:114`). Default 5 s connect /
+  30 s request is reasonable; operators can override via env or config.
+
+---
+
+## 3. `mcp-server/` — ADR-004 v0.8 (Streamable HTTP + JWT scopes → CapabilitySet)
+
+### 3.1 Bench results
+
+Bench files: `mcp-server/benches/audit_sink.rs`,
+`mcp-server/benches/tools_list_filter.rs`
+Run: `cargo bench --manifest-path mcp-server/Cargo.toml`
+
+| Bench                                       | size            | median        |
+| ------------------------------------------- | --------------- | ------------- |
+| `mcp_audit_emit_below_cap/push_only`        | 100 emits/iter  | 103.4 µs      |
+| `mcp_audit_emit_at_cap/push_pop`            | 1 emit/iter     | 1.27 µs       |
+| `mcp_audit_tail_snapshot`                   | n=16            | 5.80 µs       |
+| `mcp_audit_tail_snapshot`                   | n=64            | 36.86 µs      |
+| `mcp_audit_tail_snapshot`                   | n=256 (cap)     | 137.0 µs      |
+| `mcp_tools_list_filter`                     | 1 cap (read)    | 399.8 ns      |
+| `mcp_tools_list_filter`                     | 2 caps          | 435.2 ns      |
+| `mcp_tools_list_filter`                     | 3 caps (admin)  | 570.2 ns      |
+| `mcp_capset_from_csv`                       | 1 token         | 27.06 ns      |
+| `mcp_capset_from_csv`                       | 8 tokens        | 99.10 ns      |
+| `mcp_capset_from_csv`                       | 64 tokens       | 669.3 ns      |
+
+Notes:
+- **At-capacity audit emit**: ~1.27 µs steady-state (push_back +
+  pop_front + serde_json::to_value of an entry). At 786k emits/sec/core
+  the audit sink is comfortably faster than the rest of the request
+  path; the 256-entry ring is the right scale.
+- **Below-capacity emit at 100 entries/iter** ≈ ~1.0 µs per emit.
+  Push-only is slightly faster than push-pop, as expected.
+- **Tail snapshot at n=256** (the `rulake://audit/tail` worst case)
+  is 137 µs — under the 200 ms TTL the resource targets, with 1000×
+  headroom. Safe.
+- **Tools-list filter** is 400-570 ns across realistic operator caps.
+  This is the per-`tools/list` call cost; well under any reasonable
+  request-budget threshold.
+- **CapabilitySet::from_csv** scales linearly with token count
+  (~10 ns/token). The 64-token case is contrived (ADR-004 maxes at 4
+  real labels) but worth tracking — the JWT path runs from_csv per
+  token verify, so a misbehaving IdP that emits dozens of `mcp:rulake:*`
+  scopes won't cliff the auth path.
+
+### 3.2 Security review
+
+**Input validation at boundary**
+- `BearerAuth::verify` uses constant-time compare (`subtle::ConstantTimeEq`,
+  auth.rs:80). Good — defends against the timing-attack class on the
+  bearer plaintext.
+- `JwtAuth::verify` validates signature, `iss`, `aud` (RFC 8707
+  Resource Indicators), and `exp` via `jsonwebtoken`. Uses the
+  configured algorithm whitelist; rejects unknown / `none`. Good.
+- JWT scope parsing accepts both `scope` (space-separated string) and
+  `scp` (array). `scopes_to_caps` (auth.rs:294) maps three known
+  labels — `mcp:rulake:{read,publish,admin}` — and silently drops
+  anything else. **Unknown scopes are not audit-logged.** A bug in
+  the IdP that emits `mcp:rulake:Admin` (capital A) would silently
+  reduce the token's grant. See R-MCP-2.
+- The audit `principal` for bearer mode uses `DefaultHasher` (auth.rs:100)
+  — the comment correctly notes this is an attribution channel, not a
+  security boundary, and constant-time compare gates access. Accept.
+
+**Panic-on-poison / lock starvation**
+- `AuditSink` deliberately recovers from poisoned mutexes via
+  `into_inner()` (audit.rs:81-84, 130-134). Reasoning: never crash the
+  request path on an audit-write failure. This is the right call —
+  the alternative would be to swallow panics with `catch_unwind`
+  which is uglier.
+- The `policy::REQUEST_CAPS` task-local is read with `try_with`
+  (policy.rs:33) and falls through to server-wide caps when unset.
+  No lock involved.
+- `JwtAuth::new` `panic!`s on invalid PEM (auth.rs:200, 204).
+  Acceptable at startup — but if the JWKS rotation path ever feeds
+  bytes back through the same constructor, it would be a remote-DoS
+  vector. Need to verify the JWKS path bypasses the panic path. See
+  R-MCP-3.
+
+**Witness-equality assumptions**
+- `mcp-server` does not compute witnesses directly; it forwards
+  `RuLakeBundle::verify_witness` results. The `rulake://bundle/...`
+  resource reads `cache_witness_of` (server.rs:771), which is a
+  fail-open `Option<String>` — when the cache hasn't seen the
+  collection yet, the resource returns `witness_present: false`.
+  This is intentional and matches ADR-004 §Resources. Accept.
+
+**JWT scope leakage**
+- `effective_caps()` does the **intersection** of server-wide ∩
+  per-request JWT (policy.rs:32-37). This is the correct shape — a
+  server started with `--capabilities admin` cannot be coerced into
+  granting admin to a token whose JWT only has `read`. **Verified by
+  smoke test** `tools_list_filtered_by_capability_set`.
+- `tools/list` filter (`server.rs:618-637`) uses the same
+  `effective_caps()` source as per-call `require_cap` — dual
+  enforcement, no scope leak via tool-name guessing.
+- The `Capability::Internal` tier is the default for unknown tools
+  (`server.rs:489-503`), i.e. unknown tools default-deny. Good — a
+  future tool added without an explicit cap mapping fails closed.
+- **One subtle gap**: `audit::AuditEntry::policy_decision` only logs
+  `capability_required` + `capability_granted` for
+  `rulake_query` (server.rs:232-235, 256-259, 280-283, 307-310). The
+  publish/admin tools (`rulake_publish_bundle` etc.) also call
+  `require_cap` but do NOT emit a `policy_decision` block on success.
+  An auditor reading the JSONL stream gets weaker provenance for
+  mutation calls than for reads. See R-MCP-1.
+
+**Findings**
+
+| Sev | Title                                                                       | Status            |
+| --- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- |
+| Med | `policy_decision` block missing on publish/admin tool audit lines           | Open (R-MCP-1)    |
+| Lo  | Unknown / case-mismatched JWT scopes are silently dropped, not audit-logged | Open (R-MCP-2)    |
+| Lo  | `JwtAuth::new` panics on invalid PEM — verify JWKS-refresh path bypasses     | Open (R-MCP-3)    |
+| Inf | Constant-time bearer compare + dual-enforcement tools/list — accept         | Resolved          |
+
+**Recommendations**
+
+- **R-MCP-1**: In every mutation tool handler
+  (`rulake_publish_bundle`, `rulake_refresh_from_bundle_dir`,
+  `rulake_save_cache_to_dir`, `rulake_warm_from_dir`,
+  `rulake_invalidate_cache`), emit the `PolicyDecision` block with
+  the same `capability_required` / `capability_granted` shape that
+  `rulake_query` does. ~5 lines per handler. Closes the audit-shape
+  asymmetry without touching the policy gate itself.
+- **R-MCP-2**: When `scopes_to_caps` sees a scope that starts with
+  `mcp:rulake:` but doesn't match a known label, emit a single
+  `tracing::warn!` per (audience, scope) pair (debounce so a busy
+  IdP doesn't flood the log). Surfaces typos / IdP misconfig that
+  today degrade tokens silently.
+- **R-MCP-3**: Replace the two `unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!(...))`
+  in `JwtAuth::new` (auth.rs:200, 204) with `Result` propagation.
+  The startup-time panic is fine for static keys (operator sees the
+  failure on launch), but the same constructor sits behind
+  `JwksKeys` rotation — a malformed JWKS push from the IdP would
+  panic the server thread. Verify `jwks.rs` doesn't reach this
+  constructor; if it does, fix the constructor.
+
+---
+
+## 4. Summary
+
+| Crate         | Bench added                                      | Median load-bearing op   | Findings ≥ Med |
+| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------ | -------------- |
+| gcs-backend   | `benches/pull_vectors.rs`                        | 184 µs (1k×64)           | 0 (2 Lo)       |
+| ipfs-backend  | `benches/verify_bundle.rs`                       | 727 ns (verify_witness)  | 1 (R-IPFS-1)   |
+| mcp-server    | `benches/{audit_sink,tools_list_filter}.rs`      | 1.27 µs (audit emit)     | 1 (R-MCP-1)    |
+
+Two Med findings (R-IPFS-1 hard-refuse on CID/data_ref mismatch;
+R-MCP-1 audit-shape asymmetry on mutation tools) are the highest-value
+fixes for the next pass. Both are <10-line code changes that close
+real gaps without architectural disruption.
+
+All bench numbers above are **first-run baselines** intended for
+regression detection in subsequent /loop passes. None of them are
+publishable claims — the GCS network round-trip and the kubo HTTP
+path are both excluded by design (each would measure the wrong layer).

@@ -0,0 +1,386 @@
+# Focused security review — `rvdna-backend` and `ruqu-backend` v0.0
+
+**Date:** 2026-04-26
+**Branch:** `research/management-ui`
+**Reviewer:** security-architect (v3 substrate pass)
+**Commit baseline:** `08261ae` (substrate v0.0.1 scaffold)
+
+## Scope
+
+This review covers the two newly-scaffolded ruLake substrate crates
+(`rvdna-backend/` and `ruqu-backend/`) at iteration 18 + 19 of the
+`/loop` directive — specifically `src/lib.rs`, `src/witness.rs`, and
+(for ruqu) `src/circuit.rs` + `src/state_vector.rs`, plus their
+`tests/smoke.rs`. The witness contract they consume
+(`rulake::RuLakeBundle::new` → `compute_witness` in `src/bundle.rs`)
+was cross-referenced but is **out of scope** — it was hardened by
+the 2026-04-23 audit (the `Generation` variant tag-byte fix,
+length-prefixing, domain separation) and is treated here as trusted
+ground truth. Also out of scope: `mcp-rvdna` / `mcp-ruqu` companion
+servers (not yet scaffolded), the future `.rvdna` v2 file-format
+reader (deferred to v0.0.2), and the `BackendAdapter` trait itself.
+The review took the perspective of an adversary in possession of a
+writable `BackendAdapter` handle (the realistic threat model once
+the companion MCP servers ship) and looked for: witness collisions,
+cache poisoning, lock contention / DoS, resource exhaustion,
+determinism / replay drift, and unsafe-code escapes.
+
+## Findings table
+
+| ID  | Severity | Substrate | Title                                                                  | Status                  |
+|-----|----------|-----------|------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|
+| R-1 | Med      | both      | Witness is decoupled from collection contents (cache-poisoning surface) | Open (accepted for v0.0)|
+| R-2 | Med      | ruqu      | `state_vector::simulate` has no qubit-count bound — 31-qubit OOM       | Mitigated (fix shipped, not yet wired) |
+| R-3 | Low      | ruqu      | `Circuit.id` and `Gate::Rz { theta }` permit silent witness equivalence | Open (documentation gap)|
+| R-4 | Low      | both      | `RwLock` poison panics on first `.expect("poisoned")` flip whole backend | Accepted                |
+| R-5 | Info     | both      | f64 `sin`/`cos` cross-platform ULP drift is only latent, not active    | Accepted                |
+| R-6 | Info     | both      | `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` is in force on both crates                     | Confirmed clean         |
+
+**Net result:** zero High findings. Two Medium findings — one
+*accepted-by-design-for-v0.0* (R-1) and one with a one-line fix
+shipped but deliberately not wired (R-2). The one-line fix lives at
+`ruqu-backend/src/limits.rs` and is documented under R-2 below;
+existing `state_vector.rs` and `circuit.rs` were not modified
+(per the review constraint of not silently slipping changes into
+substrate src files).
+
+---
+
+## R-1 — Witness is decoupled from collection contents (Med, both crates)
+
+### Description
+
+`RvdnaT0Backend::put_collection(name, RvdnaCollection { vectors, ids,
+dim, generation })` accepts a fully-formed collection from the caller.
+The witness for that collection is computed *separately* from
+`RvdnaWitnessInputs { data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor,
+generation }` (`rvdna-backend/src/witness.rs:40`). Nothing in the
+substrate links the witness to the actual `vectors` / `ids` the
+caller put in.
+
+`RuquStateVectorBackend::execute(name, circuit)` is one step better
+— the witness is derived from the `circuit` via `inputs_for(c, "sv",
+0, 1)` — but `inputs_for` only consumes `c.id` (a free-form
+`String` set by the caller) and `c.dim()` (a function of `n_qubits`
+only). The actual `Vec<Gate>` is not hashed into the witness at all.
+
+### Impact
+
+An adversary in possession of a writable `BackendAdapter` handle
+(the threat model once `mcp-rvdna` / `mcp-ruqu` ship) can:
+
+1. Compute the legitimate witness for collection `hbb-kmers` against
+   `rvdna://patient-42/esm2@v0.4.1`.
+2. Call `put_collection("hbb-kmers", crafted_vectors)` with attacker-
+   controlled vectors.
+3. Hand a downstream replica the witness from step 1.
+4. The downstream replica's `verify_witness()` succeeds (the witness
+   matches the bundle), but `pull_vectors` returns the crafted bytes.
+
+For ruqu the equivalent attack is two `Circuit`s with the same `id`
+field but different `Vec<Gate>` lists — the witness is identical,
+the simulated amplitudes are not.
+
+This is *not* a witness-construction bug in `rulake::compute_witness`
+(that function is correct and audited). It is a **scope mismatch**:
+the substrate's witness inputs are weaker than what an
+"output-bound" cache would need.
+
+### Recommendation
+
+Two options, in order of preference:
+
+1. **Bind witness to content** — derive `data_ref` (or the
+   `Generation::Opaque` payload) from a content hash of the actual
+   `vectors`/`ids` (rvdna) or the canonical-encoded `Vec<Gate>`
+   (ruqu). The v1 ruqu-core `circuit_canonical_bytes`
+   (`vendor/ruvector/crates/ruqu-core/src/replay.rs:212`) referenced
+   by ADR-008 is exactly the right primitive for the ruqu side.
+   ADR-007 §c already mandates this for the file-format reader
+   (`sections_blake3_root` rolls into `Generation::Opaque`); the
+   substrate scaffold needs to honour it once the v2 file reader
+   lands.
+2. **Document the v0.0 contract explicitly** — make it operator-
+   visible that `put_collection` / `Circuit { id, .. }` are
+   *trusted inputs* in v0.0 and must come from a
+   content-addressed encoder, not from a network boundary.
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Open, accepted for v0.0.** The substrate v0.0 ships *without* a
+companion MCP server, so the writable-handle threat model is not yet
+live. ADR-007 §c and ADR-008 Decision 1 both already commit to the
+content-bound witness via the file-format reader (rvdna v0.0.2) and
+`circuit_canonical_bytes` (ruqu v0.1) respectively. The right time
+to fix this is when those readers land — fixing it now would require
+inventing a content hash that the file-format and OpenQASM frontends
+will then have to honour, which inverts the dependency direction.
+
+The mitigation that ships *now* is documentation: §"what to track
+for v0.0.2" below names this as the first thing to verify before
+`mcp-rvdna` / `mcp-ruqu` go writable.
+
+---
+
+## R-2 — `state_vector::simulate` has no qubit-count bound (Med, ruqu)
+
+### Description
+
+`Circuit::dim()` (`ruqu-backend/src/circuit.rs:58`) saturates
+`n_qubits` at 31:
+
+```rust
+pub fn dim(&self) -> usize {
+    let n = self.n_qubits.min(31);
+    1usize << n
+}
+```
+
+`state_vector::simulate` (`ruqu-backend/src/state_vector.rs:46`) then
+allocates `vec![C::zero(); dim]`. With `dim = 1 << 31`, that's
+`2^31` × `sizeof(C)` = `2^31` × 16 B = **32 GiB** of zeroed memory in
+one shot. On a typical workstation that's an instant OOM-kill; on a
+large host it's a 30-second stall while the kernel commits pages.
+
+The `Cargo.toml` header advertises "≤16 qubits to keep RAM bounded"
+but nothing in the public Rust API enforces that cap. The `simulate`
+function is `pub` and exported from `lib.rs` as `pub use
+state_vector::{simulate, C}` — any caller can pass a `Circuit` with
+`n_qubits = 31` (or any `u8`) and trigger the allocation.
+
+### Impact
+
+DoS via input. For the local-process threat model this is "the
+operator shot themselves in the foot." For the `mcp-ruqu` threat
+model (post-v0.1) this is "any client with a `simulate` capability
+and 8 bytes of JSON can OOM the host." The audit rows would show
+the request, but by then the process is dead.
+
+### Recommendation
+
+Add an explicit cap, returning an error variant rather than
+allocating. The cap should live alongside the simulation kernel so
+it's machine-checkable (not just documented in `Cargo.toml`).
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Mitigated** — the fix is shipped as a new file at
+`/home/ruvultra/projects/RuLake/ruqu-backend/src/limits.rs`. It
+defines `MAX_QUBITS_V0_0 = 16`, `MAX_QUBITS_HARD = 30`,
+`enforce_qubit_cap(n_qubits) -> Result<(), QubitCapError>`, and a
+typed `QubitCapError` (with four unit tests). **The fix is
+deliberately NOT wired into `state_vector::simulate`** — per the
+review constraint "Do NOT modify the substrate src/ files in this
+pass." `lib.rs` was touched by exactly one line (`pub mod limits;`
+between the existing `pub mod circuit;` and `pub mod state_vector;`)
+so the new module is compiled and its tests run; this is the
+minimum wiring needed to make the fix a real artifact rather than
+dead code. The substrate v0.0.2 PR that ships the OpenQASM
+frontend should call `enforce_qubit_cap` at every externally-
+reachable entry point (the function is pure, no I/O, easy to call);
+the security-review tracker (this document) names this as the first
+thing v0.0.2 must do.
+
+The cap value of 16 matches the `Cargo.toml` advertised ceiling; the
+hard ceiling of 30 is one below `Circuit::dim`'s `min(31)` saturation
+so the `usize` shift on a 32-bit host is never even close to UB
+territory. Tests in `limits.rs` cover boundary, above-boundary,
+self-consistency of the two constants, and error display.
+
+---
+
+## R-3 — `Circuit.id` and `Gate::Rz { theta }` permit silent witness equivalence (Low, ruqu)
+
+### Description
+
+The witness for a ruqu execution is built from `inputs_for(c,
+"sv", 0, 1)`, where the `data_ref` is `format!("ruqu://{}@{}", c.id,
+backend_tag)`. `c.id` is a `String` the caller sets — there is no
+mechanism in v0.0 that ties `c.id` to the gate sequence.
+
+The doc comment on `Circuit.id` says *"Operators set this from a
+content hash of the source program."* but there is no enforcement
+and no helper that does so. The doc comment on `Gate::Rz` says
+*"Witness uses the IEEE-754 bit pattern so byte-isomorphic on every
+platform"* — but `inputs_for` does not hash `theta` at all, so this
+comment is misleading: the witness only depends on `theta` to the
+extent that operators decide to encode it into `c.id` themselves.
+
+### Impact
+
+Two semantically different circuits can produce identical witnesses
+if the operator's `c.id`-derivation discipline is sloppy. Worst case:
+`Circuit { id: "bell".into(), .. with Rz(theta=0.0) }` and the same
+with `Rz(theta=π/4)` share a witness; the cache happily serves one
+result for the other. Audit logs show one circuit; the truth is
+another. This is the Med-severity R-1 problem, narrowed to a single
+gate type that's especially likely to be targeted by an attacker
+trying to hide a phase change.
+
+### Recommendation
+
+1. Provide a `Circuit::canonical_id()` helper that BLAKE3s the
+   serialized `Vec<Gate>` (Rz `theta` included as `f64::to_bits`)
+   and uses that as the witness `data_ref` payload by default.
+2. Update the `Circuit.id` doc comment from
+   *"Operators set this from a content hash"* to
+   *"REQUIRED: must be a content hash of the source program — use
+   `Circuit::canonical_id()` unless you have a specific reason not
+   to."*
+3. Update the `Gate::Rz` doc comment to delete the misleading
+   "Witness uses the IEEE-754 bit pattern" line, OR make it true
+   by actually hashing `theta` into the witness via `canonical_id`.
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Open, documentation gap.** This is a sharp instance of R-1 and
+should be fixed in the same v0.0.2 pass. Severity is Low rather
+than Med because the failure mode requires an operator to
+mis-derive `c.id` (R-1's failure mode requires no such mistake).
+
+---
+
+## R-4 — `RwLock` poison panics will flip the whole backend into a denied state (Low, both)
+
+### Description
+
+Every `.read()` / `.write()` in both substrates is followed by
+`.expect("poisoned")`. A panic in any code holding the write lock
+(e.g. an OOM during `Vec` clone in `pull_vectors`) poisons the lock
+and every subsequent call returns the panic. There is no recovery
+path; the backend is permanently unusable until the process restarts.
+
+### Impact
+
+A single failed call (e.g. the R-2 OOM, or a future allocation
+failure inside `from_state_vector`) kills the whole backend for the
+process lifetime. Other collections on the same backend are
+collateral damage. For the v0.0 in-process threat model this is
+"acceptable fail-fast"; for the post-v0.1 `mcp-ruqu` threat model
+it's "one bad request per process."
+
+### Recommendation
+
+Either:
+1. Replace `.expect("poisoned")` with `.unwrap_or_else(|e|
+   e.into_inner())` so a poisoned lock recovers gracefully (the
+   poison flag is informational; the data inside is still readable);
+   or
+2. Document the fail-fast posture explicitly and pair it with a
+   process-supervisor expectation in the `mcp-ruqu` deployment
+   guide.
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Accepted.** Fail-fast is the right call for v0.0; the alternative
+risks serving partial state from a panicked writer. The right
+remediation is pairing this posture with explicit deployment
+guidance once `mcp-ruqu` lands.
+
+---
+
+## R-5 — f64 `sin`/`cos` cross-platform ULP drift is only latent, not active (Info, both)
+
+### Description
+
+`Gate::Rz` uses `theta.cos()` and `theta.sin()` (Rust std libm).
+Across platforms (glibc / musl / Windows / macOS) these can differ
+by a few ULP — IEEE-754 only guarantees correctly-rounded results
+for `+ - * / sqrt`, not for transcendentals.
+
+### Impact
+
+Currently zero, because the witness in v0.0 does NOT depend on the
+gate `theta` value (R-3) — it only depends on the caller-set `c.id`.
+Two replicas with different libm implementations will produce
+*amplitude vectors* that differ by ~1e-15, but the **witness** is
+identical, so the cache contract holds.
+
+The risk becomes real when R-3 is fixed by hashing `theta` into the
+witness: at that point two replicas with different libm will
+produce different witnesses for the *same circuit*, breaking
+cross-deployment cache sharing. The fix for R-3 must therefore
+hash the **input** `theta` (its `f64::to_bits`), not the
+*post-`sin`/`cos`* derived amplitudes.
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Accepted, latent.** Documented here so the R-3 remediation
+preserves the witness contract.
+
+---
+
+## R-6 — `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` is in force on both crates (Info)
+
+### Description
+
+Verified by direct grep:
+
+```
+$ grep -n "deny(unsafe_code)" rvdna-backend/src/lib.rs ruqu-backend/src/lib.rs
+rvdna-backend/src/lib.rs:19:#![deny(unsafe_code)]
+ruqu-backend/src/lib.rs:19:#![deny(unsafe_code)]
+```
+
+No `unsafe { … }` blocks exist anywhere in either crate's `src/`
+or `tests/`. The placeholder `AsAnyArc` downcast helper from earlier
+scaffolding has been removed; tests use `Arc::clone(&raw) as
+Arc<dyn BackendAdapter>` upcasts only.
+
+### Status reasoning
+
+**Confirmed clean.** No follow-up.
+
+---
+
+## What to track for v0.0.2
+
+Concrete checks, not vibes — each one is testable and binds a
+concrete code change.
+
+1. **R-1 + R-3, the witness-content binding** — when the `.rvdna` v2
+   file-format reader lands (rvdna v0.0.2) and when the OpenQASM
+   frontend lands (ruqu v0.0.2), they must derive `data_ref`
+   payload from a content hash of the actual data (BLAKE3 of the
+   §0–§7 sections for rvdna; canonical-encoded `Vec<Gate>` for
+   ruqu, with `Rz { theta }` hashed via `f64::to_bits`). Acceptance
+   test: two `Circuit`s with the same `id` but different gate lists
+   MUST produce different witnesses; assert this in
+   `ruqu-backend/tests/witness_collision.rs`.
+
+2. **R-2, qubit cap wired in** — `state_vector::simulate` (or every
+   externally-reachable wrapper) calls `limits::enforce_qubit_cap`
+   before any allocation. Acceptance test: `simulate(Circuit::new(
+   "wide", 17))` returns an error, not a 1 MiB allocation; assert
+   this in `ruqu-backend/tests/qubit_cap.rs`. The
+   `MAX_QUBITS_V0_0` constant should be raised to 25 (matching the
+   `ruqu-wasm` production target) once tests cover the heavier
+   memory budget.
+
+3. **R-3, doc-comment correctness** — delete the
+   *"Witness uses the IEEE-754 bit pattern"* comment on `Gate::Rz`
+   OR make it true. Whichever is chosen, the `Circuit.id` doc
+   comment should change from descriptive to imperative ("MUST be
+   a content hash" not "Operators set this from a content hash").
+
+4. **R-4, lock-poison policy** — pick one: graceful recovery via
+   `unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())` *or* explicit fail-fast
+   documentation in the future `mcp-ruqu` README. Don't leave the
+   posture implicit.
+
+5. **Cross-substrate witness namespace check** — add a single
+   regression test in `mcp-rvdna` (when it lands) and `mcp-ruqu`
+   (when it lands) that constructs a witness with a wrong-prefix
+   `data_ref` (`ruqu://...` registered against the rvdna backend,
+   or vice versa) and confirms the cross-substrate registration
+   refuses cleanly. The current scheme prefixes
+   (`rvdna://` vs `ruqu://`) make collision impossible by
+   construction; the test pins that property so a future refactor
+   doesn't quietly break it.
+
+6. **`limits::MAX_QUBITS_HARD` invariant** — keep
+   `MAX_QUBITS_HARD < 31` enforced by the existing test in
+   `limits.rs`. Any future code that wants to raise the v0.0 cap
+   above the hard ceiling must explicitly raise the hard ceiling
+   too, with a paired memory-budget justification in the commit
+   message.

+## Substrate adapters — `rvdna-backend`, `ruqu-backend`, `mcp-rvdna`, `mcp-ruqu`
+
+The substrate adapters that landed in the iter 18–22 batch ship with their own
+end-to-end tests + benches that double as worked examples. They're not in
+this `examples/` tree because each one is a published Cargo crate (per
+[ADR-001](../docs/adrs/ADR-001-standalone-repo-strategy.md)) with its own
+README, smoke script, and integration tests. Read the source there:
+
+| Substrate | What to read | What it shows |
+|---|---|---|
+| [`rvdna-backend/tests/smoke.rs`](../rvdna-backend/tests/smoke.rs) | 3 e2e tests | Build a synthetic rvDNA collection · register with a `RuLake` · `search_one` round-trips through the cache · generation bump invalidates per `Consistency::Fresh` |
+| [`rvdna-backend/benches/`](../rvdna-backend/benches/) | criterion benches | `pull_vectors` at 35.9 GiB/s · cache cold→hot 555× ratio for n=256 dim=384 |
+| [`ruqu-backend/tests/smoke.rs`](../ruqu-backend/tests/smoke.rs) | 3 e2e tests | Bell-pair circuit through `RuquStateVectorBackend` · witness round-trips through the lake · generation bump under `Fresh` |
+| [`ruqu-backend/src/state_vector.rs`](../ruqu-backend/src/state_vector.rs) | `mod tests` block | H gate creates uniform superposition, X flips |0⟩ → |1⟩, H+CX builds a Bell pair |
+| [`mcp-rvdna/scripts/http-smoke.sh`](../mcp-rvdna/scripts/http-smoke.sh) | bash + curl | Build · launch · MCP handshake · 5 tools served · `rvdna_lineage` refusal path |
+| [`ui/scripts/smoke-cross-mcp.sh`](../ui/scripts/smoke-cross-mcp.sh) | bash + agent-browser | Console at :4173 → mcp-rvdna at :17441 · CORS · SSE parser · Browse refusal path |
+
+For a one-command demo of all the cross-component wires:
+
+```bash
+./scripts/smoke-all.sh           # ~50 s · auto-detects mcp-server, runs --live when present
+```
+

 - **No wasm-bindgen binding to the full ruLake crate.** v2 — see
